title: A/B Test Design — Rigorous Design Experimentation
content: A/B tests produce actionable results when designed with statistical rigour. Structure: 1) Hypothesis — 'If we [change], then [outcome] will [improve/decrease] because [rationale].' 2) Variants — Control (A) is current design, Treatment (B) is proposed change. Test one variable at a time. 3) Primary metric — single most important measure, must be measurable, relevant, and sensitive to the change. 4) Secondary metrics — supporting measures and guardrail metrics detecting unintended consequences. 5) Sample size — based on minimum detectable effect, baseline conversion rate, significance level (95%), and power (80%). 6) Duration — run until sample size reached, account for weekly cycles (full weeks minimum), typically 1–2 weeks minimum. Pitfalls: peeking before completion, too many variants, insensitive metrics, insufficient sample, ignoring novelty effects. When NOT to A/B test: very low traffic, ethical concerns, foundational changes, when qualitative insight is more valuable. Don't stop early on positive results.

title: Accessibility Testing — Systematic Assistive Technology Evaluation
content: Accessibility testing has four layers: 1) Automated (axe, Lighthouse, WAVE) — catches 30–40% of issues, run on every page/state, integrate into CI/CD. 2) Manual — keyboard-only navigation, screen reader walkthrough, zoom to 200% and 400%, high contrast mode, reduced motion mode. 3) Assistive technology — VoiceOver (Mac/iOS), NVDA (Windows), TalkBack (Android), Voice Control, Dragon, switch control, screen magnification. 4) User testing with disabilities — recruit participants with relevant disabilities (vision, motor, cognitive, hearing), test with their own devices, focus on real tasks. Test matrix: for each key user flow, test across keyboard only, VoiceOver, NVDA, zoom 200%, high contrast, reduced motion. Organise findings by WCAG principle (Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust) and level (A, AA, AAA). Report: description, WCAG criterion, severity, assistive tech affected, steps to reproduce, remediation. Test early and continuously — automated testing is necessary but never sufficient.

title: Click Tests — Measuring Navigation Findability
content: Click tests evaluate whether users can find information and features. Types: First-click test (where users click first for a task), Click-path test (full sequence to complete a task), Navigation test (finding items via nav structure), Five-second test (what users remember after 5 seconds of exposure). Plan structure: 1) Objective — what findability question are you answering? 2) Stimuli — screen designs or prototypes to test. 3) Tasks — clear, goal-oriented without UI hints (e.g., 'Where would you click to change your email address?'). 4) Success criteria — correct first click (target area pre-defined), time to first click, confidence rating, click distribution heat map. 5) Participants — 20–50 for quantitative results. Analysis: first-click success rate above 65% generally indicates good findability. Click distribution reveals whether confusion is widespread or isolated. Time analysis shows hesitation indicating confusion. Correlation between confidence and accuracy reveals whether the UI is deceptively confusing. Test one task per screen. Use realistic content, not lorem ipsum.

title: Heuristic Evaluation — Expert Review Against Nielsen's 10
content: Heuristic evaluation is a structured expert review against established usability principles. Nielsen's 10: 1) Visibility of system status, 2) Match between system and real world, 3) User control and freedom, 4) Consistency and standards, 5) Error prevention, 6) Recognition over recall, 7) Flexibility and efficiency of use, 8) Aesthetic and minimalist design, 9) Help users recognise and recover from errors, 10) Help and documentation. Process: define scope (screens/flows), walk through as new user, walk through as experienced user, walk through each task flow, document issues, rate severity, compile and prioritise. Issue format: heuristic violated, description, location, severity (0–4), screenshot, recommendation. Severity: 0 = not a problem, 1 = cosmetic, 2 = minor, 3 = major (fix), 4 = catastrophe (must fix before release). 3–5 evaluators find the most issues — solo evaluations miss up to 50%. Evaluate independently before comparing. Focus on real tasks, not edge cases.

title: Prototype Strategy — Matching Fidelity to the Question
content: Choose prototyping fidelity based on what you need to learn. Spectrum: Low fidelity (paper sketches, sticky notes, rough wireframes) — best for early exploration, IA, flow validation. Fast to create, easy to discard. Medium fidelity (digital wireframes, clickable prototypes, grey-box layouts) — best for interaction patterns, navigation testing, stakeholder alignment. High fidelity (pixel-perfect mockups, coded prototypes, motion prototypes) — best for visual validation, micro-interaction testing, developer handoff, usability testing. Methods: paper prototyping (manually swap screens), clickable wireframes (hotspots), interactive prototypes (stateful), coded prototypes (HTML/CSS/JS), Wizard of Oz (fake backend, real frontend), video prototypes (walkthrough animations). Decision criteria: What question are you answering? Who is the audience? How much time? How many iterations? What decisions will it inform? Prototype the riskiest assumption first. Don't over-invest before testing. Build to throw away.

title: Test Scenarios — Revealing Genuine User Behaviour
content: Test scenarios structure usability evaluation with realistic tasks. Components: Context setting (brief, realistic backstory giving the participant a reason to act — without leading), Task (specific goal, action-oriented, avoids UI terminology), Success criteria (completion yes/no, time, errors, wrong paths, assistance requests, self-reported difficulty 1–5), Observation guide (hesitations, expressions, verbal comments, navigation choices, recovery behaviour). Task types: Exploratory ('Find the return policy'), Specific ('Add a blue shirt size M to cart'), Comparative (choose between options), Open-ended (goal with multiple valid paths). Writing rules: use participant's language not product jargon, give motivation not instructions, one goal per task, don't reveal UI path in wording. Include both simple and complex tasks. Order easy to hard. Include a warm-up task. Pilot test scenarios before real sessions. Write more scenarios than needed for flexibility.

title: User Flow Diagrams — Mapping Paths and Decisions
content: User flow diagrams map how users move through a product to accomplish goals. Elements: Entry point (circle/oval), Screen/page (rectangle), Decision (diamond), Action (rounded rectangle), System process (rectangle with side bars), End point (circle with border), Connector (directional arrow). Flow types: Task flow (single linear path for specific task), User flow (multiple paths based on user type or choice), Wire flow (flow combined with wireframe thumbnails). Process: 1) Define the goal. 2) Identify entry points. 3) Map the happy path first. 4) Add decision points and branches. 5) Map error paths and recovery. 6) Mark exit points. 7) Note background system actions. Annotations: screen names, decision criteria, error conditions, system events, time delays, async processes. Best practices: one flow per user goal, start with happy path then add complexity, include error and edge case paths, keep diagrams readable, use consistent notation, label every arrow with trigger/action.

title: Wireframe Specifications — Content Priority and Behaviour
content: Wireframe specs define content priority, component placement, behaviour, and responsive considerations. Content blocks: headers/navigation, hero/feature areas, content sections, forms/input areas, footers. Annotations include: content priority numbers (what loads first), interaction notes (click/hover behaviour), dynamic content indicators (personalised, data-driven), responsive behaviour, accessibility notes. Content specs: heading hierarchy (H1–H3), approximate character counts, image aspect ratios, required vs optional content, content source (static, CMS, API). Fidelity levels: Sketch (hand-drawn boxes), Low-fi (grey boxes with labels), Mid-fi (realistic layout with placeholders), Annotated (mid-fi plus detailed behaviour specs). Conventions: grey/black/white only (no colour decisions), X-box for images, wavy lines for text, real labels for navigation and buttons. Focus on content hierarchy, not visual design. Show multiple states (empty, loading, populated, error). Include responsive breakpoint versions. Get content strategy input early — wireframes without real content strategy are just decoration.
